era_context: The early 10th century CE marks the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate in the Islamic world, the consolidation of the Song Dynasty's rise in China following the collapse of the Tang, the Viking Age in full swing with Norse settlement and raiding across Europe and the Atlantic, and the emergence of feudal structures in Western Europe. In Mesoamerica, the Classic Maya collapse has long since occurred; the Postclassic period is underway with new polities rising in the Yucatan.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record underexplored in training data)
Southeast Asia (sparse contemporary documentation in Western sources)
Pacific Islands (oral traditions not well integrated into chronological records)

events.title: Death of Zhu Wen; Proclamation of Later Liang Dynasty
events.description: Zhu Wen, the warlord who had seized control of northern China and proclaimed himself emperor of the Later Liang Dynasty in 907, died in 912 according to most sources, but his consolidation of power and elimination of Tang remnants occurred in the years immediately preceding. By 906, Zhu Wen had effectively ended the Tang Dynasty (which formally fell in 907) and was establishing the military-administrative apparatus of Later Liang. His death marked a critical transition point in the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period, a era of extreme fragmentation in China lasting until 979. Zhu Wen's rise exemplified the militarization of Chinese politics and the shift from aristocratic to military-bureaucratic power.
key_figures: Zhu Wen (Emperor Taizu of Later Liang, r. 907–912)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang, 11th century
Official histories of the Five Dynasties period (Wudai Shi)

events.certainty_note: The exact year of Zhu Wen's consolidation is debated; 906–907 marks the transition. Later Liang's formal proclamation is dated 907, but Zhu Wen's effective control was established by 906.

events.title: Fatimid Caliphate Consolidation in North Africa
events.region: North Africa (Ifriqiya, modern Tunisia and surrounding regions)
events.description: By 906, the Fatimid Caliphate, founded in 909 according to traditional dating but with roots in the late 9th century, was consolidating its power in North Africa. The Fatimids represented a major Shi'a challenge to Sunni Abbasid authority and would eventually expand to control Egypt and the Levant. In 906, the Fatimid movement was in its formative phase, with the Mahdi (the messianic figure) recognized by followers, though the formal proclamation of the caliphate occurred in 909. This period saw intense religious and political competition in the western Islamic world.
key_figures: Abdullah al-Mahdi (founder of Fatimid movement, recognized as Mahdi)
Abu Muhammad al-Qasim al-Rida (early Fatimid leader)

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: The Fatimid movement's early chronology is contested. Traditional sources place the Mahdi's recognition and early organization in the late 9th century; 906 is assigned here as a marker of consolidation, but exact dating is uncertain.

events.title: Viking Raids and Settlement in the North Atlantic
events.region: North Atlantic, Scandinavia, British Isles, Iceland, Greenland
events.description: The early 10th century was a period of intense Viking expansion and settlement. By 906, Norse settlers had established permanent communities in Iceland (settled from c. 874 onwards) and were beginning to explore and settle Greenland (formal settlement c. 985, but exploration and temporary camps earlier). Viking raids on Western Europe, the British Isles, and coastal regions continued unabated. The Norse were also active in the eastern Baltic and had established trade routes to the Caspian Sea and beyond. This period represents the height of Viking Age expansion and influence.
key_figures: Erik the Red (explorer, though his Greenland settlement was later, c. 985)
Various unnamed Viking jarls and chieftains

sources.name: Landnámabók (Book of Settlements), Icelandic saga tradition, compiled 12th–13th century
Eiríks saga rauða (Erik the Red's Saga), 13th century compilation
Archaeological evidence from Viking settlements in Iceland and Greenland

events.certainty_note: Viking settlement patterns and dates are well-established through archaeology and saga traditions, though specific events in 906 are not individually documented. The broader pattern of expansion is confirmed.

events.title: Khmer Empire Stability and Temple Construction
events.description: The Khmer Empire under the Angkorian period (9th–15th centuries) was in a phase of relative stability and cultural flourishing in the early 10th century. King Yasovarman I (r. 889–910) was engaged in extensive temple construction and administrative consolidation. The construction of Angkor Wat and related temple complexes, while primarily associated with later kings (especially Suryavarman II in the 12th century), had roots in the architectural and religious traditions being developed during this period. The Khmer were developing sophisticated hydraulic systems and expanding their territorial control.
key_figures: Yasovarman I (King of Khmer Empire, r. 889–910)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Angkor and surrounding sites
Chinese historical records (Zhou Daguan's later account, 13th century)

events.certainty_note: Yasovarman I's reign is well-attested in inscriptions. Specific events in 906 are not individually documented, but the broader context of his rule and temple construction is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of the Fatimid Caliphate's founding (909) has been questioned by some modern scholars who argue for earlier organizational roots in the late 9th century, making the distinction between 'movement' and 'caliphate' somewhat artificial. Additionally, the significance of events in 906 specifically is often overstated in general histories; the year itself is not marked by dramatic events in most regions, but rather represents a point within broader processes of change (Tang collapse, Fatimid consolidation, Viking expansion). The 'Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms' period in China is often portrayed as purely chaotic, but recent scholarship emphasizes continuities in administrative practice and cultural development despite political fragmentation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 906 is heavily weighted toward East Asia (China) and the Islamic world, where written chronicles and administrative records survive. Western European sources are sparse and often focused on monastic or royal courts. Southeast Asian history relies heavily on inscriptions and archaeology, with gaps in year-by-year documentation. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and the Pacific are virtually absent from contemporary written sources, though archaeological evidence exists. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history must be inferred from material evidence or later compilations. Chinese sources, while abundant, are often compiled centuries after events and reflect the biases of later dynasties.

graph_edges.note: Zhu Wen's consolidation directly led to the formal proclamation of Later Liang Dynasty in 907
Fatimid movement consolidation in 906 led to formal caliphate proclamation in 909
Viking exploration and settlement patterns in the 10th century led to Erik the Red's Greenland settlement
Khmer temple construction traditions of the early 10th century contributed to the later construction of Angkor Wat under Suryavarman II
